channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Burying their heads in the ash
item.description: UK tobacco policy risks burying its head in the ash if it ignores the role safer nicotine products can play in reducing smoking. Cigarettes remain the most harmful option, yet restrictions on vaping, pouches and other smoke-free alternatives may make switching less attractive for adult smokers. Harm reduction does not mean denying risk — it means recognising that not all risks are equal. Public health should focus on moving people away from combustion, not making safer choices harder. (Published on June 26, 2026bythecritic.co.uk)

item.title: The Rest of the Story: Tobacco and Alcohol News Analysis and Commentary: Another Sham E-Cigarette Study is Retracted; Can We Trust Any of this Literature?
item.description: Poorly designed e-cigarette studies can do real damage when weak methods are turned into strong public health claims. If research fails to account for smoking history, product use patterns or the difference between association and causation, it risks misleading policymakers and the public. Vaping should be scrutinised, but the evidence must be held to serious scientific standards. Harm reduction policy should be guided by robust data, not studies that confuse risk rather than clarify it. (Published on June 26, 2026byblogspot.com)

item.title: The Cleveland Clinic's very bad advice
item.description: Smokers deserve better than advice that treats vaping as no different from cigarettes. While no nicotine product is risk-free, public health guidance should clearly distinguish between combustible tobacco and smoke-free alternatives that reduce exposure to toxicants. When trusted institutions blur that distinction, smokers may be discouraged from switching to lower-risk options. Accurate risk communication is not a concession to nicotine — it is a necessary part of reducing smoking-related harm. (Published on June 26, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: New Zealand's accelerating smoking decline: lessons for tobacco harm reduction
item.description: New Zealand’s experience shows how quickly progress on smoking can become complicated when policy loses sight of harm reduction. Restrictive approaches may aim to reduce tobacco use, but they can also leave smokers with fewer practical routes away from combustible cigarettes. If lower-risk alternatives are limited, stigmatised or made harder to access, public health gains may be weakened rather than strengthened. Effective policy should reduce smoking-related harm while keeping realistic, regulated options available for people who need them. (Published on June 26, 2026bythelancet.com)

item.title: GFN.TV Interviews #90 | BLACK MARKET MAJORITY | 76% of Global Vaping Is Now Illicit
item.description: If most global vaping now sits outside legal markets, prohibition is not controlling nicotine use — it is losing control of it. Illicit supply leaves consumers with weaker safeguards, less product transparency and fewer age controls, while cigarettes often remain legally available. The lesson is straightforward: demand does not disappear when safer products are banned or overrestricted. Harm reduction needs regulated access, not black-market dependence. (Published on June 26, 2026bygfn.tv)
